steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The relative clause has one main verb: encourages. Its subject is whose work. Between the subject and the main verb sits a long description about the richness of Browne's poems and historical importance. The blank must fill the verb slot in that middle description without creating a second independent verb that would compete with encourages.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — highlights: a finite verb. Inserting it creates two competing finite verbs — 'highlights' and 'encourages' — in the same clause with no conjunction, making a run-on. ✗
  • Choice B — highlighting: a present participle. It modifies whose work and describes the manner of the work, leaving encourages as the sole main verb of the clause. Grammatically correct. ✓
  • Choice C — has highlighted: a finite verb in the present perfect. Same problem as A — two finite verbs in the same clause with no conjunction. ✗
  • Choice D — is highlighting: a finite verb in the present progressive. Same run-on error as A and C. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
